description: Founded in 1912 and helmed by third-generation chef Yoshihiro Murata, Kikunoi Honten is one of Japan's most celebrated kaiseki institutions. Set in a serene garden estate near Kodaiji Temple and Yasaka Shrine in Higashiyama, the restaurant exemplifies the ryotei tradition at its most complete — seasonal ingredients, exquisite tableware, and immaculate hospitality. Murata has done more than almost any chef alive to promote Japanese cuisine internationally, including serving as a key figure in its UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage designation. A benchmark kaiseki experience in Kyoto.
highlights: Three-Michelin-star kaiseki; historic garden estate near Yasaka Shrine; internationally celebrated chef Yoshihiro Murata; seasonal Kyoto ingredients with subtle Western inflections.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; World of Mouth; Condé Nast Traveler; 50 Best Discovery

description: Hyotei is one of the most storied dining destinations on earth — a 450-year-old kaiseki ryotei that began as a humble teahouse on the approach to Nanzenji Temple and has been in continuous operation through 15 generations of the Takahashi family. Set in a moss-garden compound of individual tea rooms with thatched roofs and stone-channelled streams, it holds three Michelin stars and is consistently cited among Asia's greatest restaurants. The signature soft-boiled Hyotei egg, served since the restaurant's founding, remains the most quietly famous dish in Kyoto.
highlights: Three Michelin stars; 450-year history; stunning tea-room and garden setting near Nanzenji; 15 generations of family ownership; signature Hyotei egg.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; 50 Best Discovery; World of Mouth; Condé Nast Traveler; Time Out

description: Gion Sasaki is Kyoto's most theatrical three-Michelin-star restaurant, led by the charismatic Chef Hiroshi Sasaki from an open counter in the heart of Gion. Unlike the hushed formality of many Kyoto kaiseki houses, Sasaki cultivates a lively, interactive atmosphere — guests watch every dish being assembled, and the chef's personality is integral to the experience. The 19-seat counter is notoriously difficult to book, and the cooking blends deep tradition with playful invention. A unique and unforgettable evening.
highlights: Three Michelin stars; open-kitchen kappo counter; charismatic chef Hiroshi Sasaki; one of Kyoto's most sought-after bookings; Gion location.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; 50 Best Discovery; Condé Nast Traveler; World of Mouth

description: One of Kyoto's oldest kaiseki restaurants, Isshisoden Nakamura has been in the Nakamura family since 1827 and carries the rare distinction of 'isshi soden' — a tradition of passing a single secret recipe exclusively to one heir per generation. Sixth-generation chef Motokazu Nakamura upholds this philosophy through dishes of quiet, refined authority: white miso zoni, sea bream, and seasonal compositions that feel simultaneously ancient and immediate. Three Michelin stars; located near Nishiki Market.
highlights: Three Michelin stars; nearly 200-year history; isshi soden culinary lineage; white miso zoni signature dish; central Kyoto location near Nishiki Market.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; Savor Japan; AutoReserve; Japan Wonder Travel Blog

description: Mizai is one of the most singular dining experiences in Japan — a three-Michelin-star kaiseki counter hidden within the grounds of Maruyama Park, steps from Yasaka Shrine, with no signage and access by reservation only. Chef Hitoshi Ishihara, inspired by Zen teachings and the wabicha spirit of the tea ceremony, serves all guests simultaneously in a single evening sitting, creating a communal, almost meditative atmosphere. The name means 'not yet here' in Zen — an expression of Ishihara's belief in perpetual self-improvement. Photography is discouraged.
highlights: Three Michelin stars; hidden in Maruyama Park; Zen-inspired single-sitting format; all guests dine simultaneously; listed by 50 Best Discovery.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; 50 Best Discovery; Savor Japan; AutoReserve

description: Kichisen is Kyoto's most private and exclusive three-Michelin-star restaurant — a cha-kaiseki house near Shimogamo Shrine and the ancient Tadasu no Mori forest that accepts reservations only through hotel concierges and provides counter seating for just five guests at a time. Chef Yoshimi Tanigawa's dishes are works of refined beauty, each assembled with an almost devotional attention to ingredient quality, seasonality, and tableware. The combination of location, intimacy, and culinary mastery makes Kichisen uniquely compelling for the serious luxury traveller.
highlights: Two-to-three Michelin stars across editions; reservation by hotel concierge only; maximum five counter seats; stunning forest setting near Shimogamo Shrine; cha-kaiseki tradition.
restaurant_setting: Historic garden setting near sacred forest
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; My Concierge Japan; Condé Nast Traveler; Japan Wonder Travel Blog

description: Soujiki Nakahigashi is one of Japan's most distinctive kaiseki restaurants — a two-Michelin-star counter where chef Hisao Nakahigashi personally forages wild herbs, mushrooms, and vegetables from the mountains northeast of Kyoto before each service, then cooks them with barely-there seasoning that amplifies rather than transforms nature. The open kitchen and charcoal grill create an intimate, energetic atmosphere. The philosophy of sojiki — eating simply and close to the land — produces results of extraordinary depth, with no luxury ingredients in sight.
highlights: Two Michelin stars; legendary foraging philosophy; chef-founder Hisao Nakahigashi; open charcoal-grill counter; wild and seasonal ingredients; Michelin Green Star for sustainability.
restaurant_setting: Intimate counter with open charcoal grill
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; 50 Best Discovery; Smith Hotels; World of Mouth; Condé Nast Traveler

description: Ogata is one of Kyoto's most influential and hard-to-book kaiseki restaurants — a deeply minimalist counter where chef Toshiro Ogata pursues an almost philosophical purism, stripping every dish to its essential nature. The interior design echoes the food: spare, refined, and immaculate, with a particular beauty in the relationship between tableware and ingredient. Two Michelin stars and consistently cited by insiders as one of the city's most intellectually compelling dining experiences. Bookings require contacts in Kyoto and are notoriously difficult for walk-in international visitors.
highlights: Two Michelin stars; extreme minimalism in both food and design; chef Toshiro Ogata; notoriously difficult to book; one of Kyoto's most revered insider destinations.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; Smith Hotels; World of Mouth; 50 Best Discovery

description: Kikunoi Roan is the more intimate counterpart to the Kikunoi Honten flagship — a two-Michelin-star kaiseki restaurant in the heart of Gion that brings chef Yoshihiro Murata's seasonal cuisine to a slightly more accessible setting, with counter seating that allows guests to watch the kitchen at work. The menu reflects Murata's signature philosophy: deep respect for Kyoto tradition, with subtle Western inflections — a drizzle of olive oil, a shaving of truffle — that feel natural rather than gratuitous. An ideal entry point into the Kikunoi universe.
highlights: Two Michelin stars; Gion location; chef Yoshihiro Murata; counter seating; seasonal kaiseki with subtle Western touches.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; Savor Japan; Global Trip Diaries

description: Gion Nishikawa is a benchmark of Kyoto kaiseki's quieter, more introspective tradition — a two-Michelin-star restaurant in Gion where chef Masayoshi Nishikawa applies the motto 'true flavour is subtle flavour' to ten exquisitely considered courses, drawing on 70 seasonally selected ingredients and water sourced from Yasaka Shrine for his dashi. The sukiya-style interior, with its sliding screens and natural wood, creates an atmosphere of refined calm that perfectly frames the cuisine. One of the most approachable of Kyoto's serious kaiseki counters for international visitors.
highlights: Two Michelin stars; Gion location; sukiya-style interior; water sourced from Yasaka Shrine; chef Masayoshi Nishikawa.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; Japan Wonder Travel Blog; Global Trip Diaries; My Concierge Japan

description: Gion Matayoshi is a quiet revelation — a two-Michelin-star kaiseki restaurant in Gion where Okinawan-born chef Kazutomo Matayoshi brings an outsider's clarity to Kyoto tradition, producing deeply seasonal dishes of remarkable elegance alongside a sake selection considered one of the finest in the city. The handcrafted interior — minimalist furnishings, precise lighting — and the intimacy of the counter create a dining experience that is both dignified and dramatic. A cult favourite among serious diners and increasingly sought after by international visitors.
highlights: Two Michelin stars; chef Kazutomo Matayoshi; exceptional sake programme; handcrafted minimalist interior; intimate Gion counter.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; Japan Wonder Travel Blog; Global Trip Diaries

description: Sanso Kyoyamato is Kyoto kaiseki at its most cinematically beautiful — a newly-promoted two-Michelin-star ryotei in Gion housed in a historic building surrounded by seasonal gardens, where the decor is reset entirely with each change of season. The restaurant has operated since 1877 and is currently led by seventh-generation proprietress Junko Sakaguchi — winner of the 2025 Michelin Service Award — who welcomes guests in kimono with warmth and fluent English, making this one of the most accessible and atmospherically rich kaiseki experiences in Kyoto.
highlights: Two Michelin stars (2025 promotion); historic garden building; 2025 Michelin Service Award; English-speaking proprietress; seasonal seasonal décor rotation.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; Savor Japan

description: Kodaiji Wakuden is the flagship ryotei of the prestigious Wakuden group, set in a purpose-built building inspired by tea-ceremony arbours adjacent to the historic Kodaiji Temple in Higashiyama. A two-Michelin-star kaiseki house, it is known for bringing through exceptional young chefs and for the elegance of its seasonal compositions — the Taiza crab courses in winter are legendary. The Wakuden group also holds a Michelin Green Star for its own organic rice farm in Kyotango. The combination of setting, culinary quality, and institutional prestige makes this one of Kyoto's most coveted bookings.
highlights: Two Michelin stars; adjacent to Kodaiji Temple; tea-ceremony-inspired architecture; seasonal kaiseki with celebrated Taiza crab courses; Wakuden group Green Star.
restaurant_setting: Tea-ceremony-inspired dining rooms near Kodaiji Temple
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; Savor Japan; Global Trip Diaries; My Concierge Japan

description: Ryosho is one of the most remarkable recent arrivals in the Kyoto kaiseki firmament — earning two Michelin stars within its first year of opening in 2022. Located in Gion, the restaurant quickly became one of the city's most talked-about reservations for its understated approach to seasonal Japanese cuisine: minimal seasoning, premium ingredients, and a quiet, almost meditative dining room atmosphere. A rare combination of instant critical acclaim and genuine culinary substance.
highlights: Two Michelin stars awarded within first year; Gion location; minimalist kaiseki philosophy; seasonal ingredients; one of Kyoto's fastest-rising restaurants.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; Savor Japan; My Concierge Japan

description: Jean-Georges at The Shinmonzen is one of Kyoto's most compelling luxury hotel dining rooms — the Kyoto outpost of Jean-Georges Vongerichten's global empire, set inside a nine-suite boutique hotel designed by Tadao Ando on the scenic Shirakawa River in Gion. The restaurant was awarded a Michelin star in 2025 and offers a menu of contemporary French cuisine built around exceptional local Kyoto produce, with a wine cellar of over 3,000 bottles. The combination of Ando's architecture, riverside setting, and the Jean-Georges name makes this one of the city's most design-conscious dining destinations.
highlights: One Michelin star (2025); Tadao Ando-designed hotel on the Shirakawa River; Jean-Georges Vongerichten; 3,000-bottle wine cellar; Gion address.
restaurant_setting: Luxury hotel dining room on Shirakawa River
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; Smith Hotels; Mr & Mrs Smith; Condé Nast Traveler; Monocle

description: LURRA° is Kyoto's most internationally celebrated chef-driven restaurant — a one-Michelin-star innovative dining room in a renovated townhouse in Higashiyama, where American-born chef Jacob Kear (ex-Noma Copenhagen) cooks exclusively over fire, foraging daily from the mountains surrounding Kyoto. The 12-course tasting menu is a seamless fusion of Nordic technique and Japanese ingredient culture, with a beverage programme curated by a co-founder who trained as both sommelier and mixologist. Widely cited by international food media as one of Asia's most exciting dining destinations.
highlights: One Michelin star; ex-Noma chef Jacob Kear; fire-only cooking; daily foraging; Higashiyama townhouse; listed by 50 Best Discovery; exceptional beverage programme.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; 50 Best Discovery; World of Mouth; Condé Nast Traveler; Eater

description: Cenci is Kyoto's finest Italian restaurant — a one-Michelin-star dining room in a century-old renovated machiya near Heian Shrine in Okazaki, where Kyoto-born chef Ken Sakamoto applies years of Italian training to a hyper-seasonal 10-course menu built entirely on domestic Japanese ingredients. The restaurant was ranked 20th in Asia's 50 Best Restaurants in 2023, propelling it to a global audience. The entrance — a red-brick tunnel hand-laid by Sakamoto himself — signals the restaurant's highly personal, craft-driven character. One of the most original and internationally recognised dining addresses in Kyoto.
highlights: One Michelin star; 20th in Asia's 50 Best Restaurants 2023; chef Ken Sakamoto; Italian technique with Japanese ingredients; hand-built red-brick entrance; Okazaki machiya.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; Asia's 50 Best Restaurants; 50 Best Discovery; Condé Nast Traveler; Eater

description: Muromachi Wakuden is the most accessible and centrally located expression of the Wakuden kaiseki tradition — a one-Michelin-star and Michelin Green Star restaurant set in a beautifully preserved traditional machiya townhouse in the Karasuma Oike area. The group runs its own organic rice farm in Kyotango, and the rice — planted and harvested with staff participation — is the centrepiece of every meal. A rare combination of culinary pedigree, sustainability commitment, and Kyoto architectural beauty in a central, walkable address.
highlights: One Michelin star; Michelin Green Star for sustainability; own organic rice farm; historic machiya setting; central Karasuma Oike address.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; Savor Japan; byFood; Michelin Green Star listing

description: Kiyama is one of Kyoto's most acclaimed debut restaurants — earning a Michelin star in its opening year, an achievement that generated substantial media attention. The restaurant sits above a well connected to the same underground stream that feeds the Imperial Palace, and chef Yoshiro uses naturally soft water as the foundation for his dashi and cooking. The result is kaiseki of exceptional clarity, with standout seasonal compositions and a thoughtful wine selection that includes both sake and European bottles. Set on a quiet Nakagyo street, it is intimate, welcoming, and internationally minded.
highlights: Michelin star from opening year; underground well water used for dashi; chef Yoshiro; exceptional dashi technique; seasonal kaiseki; English-friendly.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; World of Mouth; byFood; Wanderlog; My Concierge Japan

description: Sumibi Kappo Ifuki is one of Gion's most admired live-fire kappo restaurants — a two-Michelin-star counter where the chef cooks directly over charcoal in front of guests, producing seasonal Japanese dishes with a distinctive depth and intensity that purely induction-based kitchens cannot replicate. The restaurant is a cult favourite among Kyoto insiders and has built a strong international following for its combination of dramatic theatre, culinary precision, and Gion atmosphere. Reservations require significant advance planning.
highlights: Two Michelin stars; charcoal-fire kappo cooking in front of guests; Gion address; cult insider reputation; theatrical counter experience.
restaurant_setting: Intimate counter with open charcoal grill
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; Savor Japan; My Concierge Japan

description: Miyamasou is Kyoto's most remote and romantically positioned luxury dining destination — a two-Michelin-star and Michelin Green Star inn and restaurant in the mountains of Ohara, over an hour from the city centre, where the Nakahigashi family has cooked with foraged mountain ingredients since the 1970s. The setting — a traditional farmhouse surrounded by forest streams and moss — is unlike anything in the city, and the kaiseki is shaped entirely by what the mountains provide each day. An essential pilgrimage for serious food travellers willing to make the journey.
highlights: Two Michelin stars; Michelin Green Star; legendary mountain inn setting in Ohara; Nakahigashi family foraging tradition; one of Japan's most atmospheric dining pilgrimages.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; Savor Japan; Condé Nast Traveler; 50 Best Discovery

description: Shimmonzen Yonemura is Kyoto's most inventive fusion kaiseki restaurant — a one-Michelin-star destination in Higashiyama where chef Masayasu Yonemura weaves French, Chinese, and traditional Japanese techniques into a coherent and deeply personal cuisine. Dishes like foie gras bourguignon with clams and playful East-West compositions attract an international, design-conscious clientele who might find a purely traditional kaiseki too austere. An ideal destination for luxury diners who want Kyoto's culinary ambition with a more global palate.
highlights: One Michelin star; playful fusion of French, Chinese, and Japanese; chef Masayasu Yonemura; international clientele; Higashiyama address.
restaurant_setting: Contemporary dining room in historic area
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; Global Trip Diaries; My Concierge Japan

description: Noguchi Tsunagu is the highly anticipated successor restaurant to the former two-Michelin-star Kyotenjin Noguchi — one of Kyoto's most celebrated and near-impossible-to-book addresses. Chef Daisuke Yoshida carries the Noguchi legacy into a more accessible format, offering elegant kappo-style kaiseki with an English-language menu, making this one of the few serious Kyoto destinations that genuinely welcomes international guests without a local intermediary. The signature A5-grade sirloin nikusui in Kyoto-style broth is celebrated by food media worldwide.
highlights: One Michelin star (2021–2025); successor to legendary two-star Kyotenjin Noguchi; English-language menu; internationally acclaimed; A5 sirloin nikusui signature dish.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; OMAKASE JapanEatinerary; byFood; Condé Nast Traveler

description: Koke is one of the most striking recent additions to Kyoto's international dining scene — a one-Michelin-star restaurant rooted in Spanish cuisine, where chef Yusaku Nakamura, trained in Japan and Southeast Asia, builds a concept centred on water and fire: courtyard-well water and wood charcoal from a visible fireplace. The restaurant is set in a quiet Kyoto alleyway with a view of a beautiful private courtyard, and the wine pairings — offered in selections of six or nine — are thoughtfully curated. An English-language menu ensures accessibility for international guests.
highlights: One Michelin star (2024–2025); Spanish-influenced; wood-fire cooking; courtyard well water; English-language menu; beautiful alley courtyard setting.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; OMAKASE JapanEatinerary

description: Mankamerou is Kyoto's most historically unique luxury dining address — a two-Michelin-star restaurant specialising in 'yusoku ryori', the ancient imperial court cuisine of the Heian period, which is offered nowhere else in the world at this level. Ten generations of the Kanamean family have preserved these 1,000-year-old recipes and ceremonial serving traditions, producing a dining experience that is simultaneously archaeological and deeply moving. Located near Nijo Castle, the restaurant is as much a cultural institution as a restaurant — the definitive address for diners who want to understand what Kyoto cuisine truly is.
highlights: Two Michelin stars; unique 1,000-year-old imperial court cuisine; ten generations of the Kanamean family; located near Nijo Castle; one-of-a-kind cultural dining experience.
sources: Michelin Guide 2025; Savor Japan; Japan Wonder Travel Blog; My Concierge Japan
